planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in strongly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained, undisturbed forest soil in deep shade; trailing arbutus depends on specific mycorrhizal fungi and is highly sensitive to root disturbance. Only plant nursery-propagated stock — collection from the wild is illegal in many states.

directions: Plant in strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, perfectly drained, undisturbed woodland soil in deep to partial shade; trailing arbutus is the beloved state flower of Massachusetts ('Mayflower') and one of the most fragrant of all native wildflowers — the clusters of small, waxy, white to pink flowers in late winter to early spring have an intense, sweet fragrance disproportionate to their modest size. The plant is notoriously difficult to grow — it requires specific mycorrhizal fungal associates, strongly acidic soil, excellent drainage, and undisturbed native woodland conditions. It will not survive in average garden soil or compost-amended beds. A protected species in several states; never collect from the wild. Suitable only for established, undisturbed acidic woodland gardens in cool climates where its specific soil requirements already exist naturally.

planting.note: Plant divisions carefully in spring in moist, humus-rich, strongly acidic, undisturbed forest soil in partial to deep shade; like most pyrolas, American wintergreen depends on specific mycorrhizal fungi and resists transplanting and cultivation in standard garden conditions.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, strongly acidic, well-drained, undisturbed forest soil in deep to partial shade; American wintergreen is a native evergreen herb of cool, moist, acidic woodland floors in the northern hardwood and mixed conifer forests of eastern North America. The rounded, leathery, glossy dark-green leaves form attractive low rosettes that persist through winter. The raceme of nodding, waxy white flowers in summer resembles a miniature lily-of-the-valley flower stalk. Like its relatives in the family Pyrolaceae (now Ericaceae), it depends on mycorrhizal fungi and resists conventional cultivation — it is best appreciated in its natural habitat or in undisturbed, established woodland gardens with intact acidic duff. The plant has a long history of herbal use in traditional medicine, with the leaves containing substances similar to aspirin. Collect only nursery-propagated stock; do not disturb wild populations.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall immediately after collection for spring germination; rue anemone grows from small, tuberous roots in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade. Divide tubers in fall or early spring. Note: previously placed in Anemonella thalictroides, now classified as Enemion biternatum.

directions: Plant in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, loose woodland soil in partial to full shade; rue anemone is a beloved native spring ephemeral of the eastern deciduous forest, blooming in the same time window as bloodroot and trout lilies in early spring when the woodland floor is sunlit before canopy leaf-out. The delicate, white to pale pink flowers with their anemone-like appearance and rue-like blue-green compound leaves make it one of the most charming of the spring wildflowers. It grows from tiny, tuberous roots that store energy through the summer dormancy period. Unlike true anemones, rue anemone is not aggressive and spreads very slowly and politely, making it an ideal, well-behaved inhabitant of the cultivated shade garden. Combine with Virginia bluebells, spring beauty, trillium, and wild ginger for a classic eastern woodland spring garden.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall immediately after ripening; trillium seeds require 18+ months of warm+cold stratification before germination — plants take 5–7 years from seed to first flower. Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade. Rhizome division in late summer is faster.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained woodland soil in partial to full shade; prairie trillium is a distinctive sessile trillium (flowers without stems, sitting directly on the leaves) native to the moist, rich bottomland forests, floodplain woodlands, and upland mesic forests of the central US Midwest — from Michigan and Ohio west to Iowa and south to Arkansas. The dark maroon-purple petals and strongly mottled leaves with brownish-green markings (resembling a snake skin) distinguish it from other trilliums. It grows naturally in rich alluvial forest soils and tolerates slightly more open conditions than some woodland trilliums. Like all trilliums, it takes many years from seed to flowering and is legally protected in several states — purchase only nursery-propagated plants from ethical native plant nurseries. Forms slow-spreading colonies over decades.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall; like all trilliums, drooping trillium takes 5–7 years from seed to first flower and requires 18+ months of stratification for germination. Plant in moist, humus-rich, calcium-rich to neutral, well-drained soil under deciduous trees in partial to full shade.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, calcium-rich to slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; drooping trillium is a pedicellate trillium (flower on a stem, held above the leaf whorl) with large, pure white flowers that nod downward or horizontally — the drooping or bending of the flower stalk is the characteristic that gives it both common and scientific names (flexipes = bent foot). It grows naturally in rich, calcium-rich, mesic forests — particularly common in beech-maple forests over limestone — across the central and upper Midwest. One of the taller and more robust pedicellate trilliums, with large, broad leaves and fragrant white flowers that may age to pink or red-purple as they age. The attractive berries in late summer are eaten by birds and small mammals that disperse the seeds. Plant only nursery-propagated stock; wild collection is illegal in most states.
